questionText: A particle moves so that its position at time $t$ is $x = t$, $y = e^{-t}$. Find the gradient of its path and its exact speed at the instant $t = 0$.

steps.description: Differentiate x with respect to t
steps.explanation: Since $x=t$, the horizontal position increases at a steady rate of $1$; this is the horizontal velocity.

steps.description: Differentiate y with respect to t
steps.explanation: By the chain rule, $e^{-t}$ differentiates to $-e^{-t}$: multiply by the derivative of the exponent $-t$, which is $-1$.

steps.description: Form the gradient of the path
steps.explanation: Dividing the vertical rate by the horizontal rate gives the slope of the path at any time $t$.
At $t=0$, $e^{0}=1$, so the path is sloping downwards with gradient $-1$ at that instant.

steps.description: Identify the path in Cartesian form
steps.explanation: Because $x$ equals $t$ exactly, replacing $t$ by $x$ shows the path is the exponential decay curve $y=e^{-x}$, which confirms a downward slope everywhere.
Speed combines the two velocity components using Pythagoras to give the magnitude of the total velocity.
Both components are found by putting $t=0$ into the derivatives.
Squaring removes the negative sign, so both terms contribute a positive $1$.
This is the exact speed at $t=0$, left as a surd rather than a rounded decimal.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: At $t=0$ the gradient of the path is $\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-1$ and the exact speed is $\sqrt{2}$ units per unit time.
